title: Vision of St. Paul
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00151. U-M encoded Eugen Kölbing's 1896 first printing of an anonymous Middle English prose Vision of St. Paul from British Library, Add MS 10036, ff. 81r-85r. The encoding includes the series and article headings, Kölbing's short German introduction, Middle English text on printed pp. 134-137 (source lines 1-101), six inline notes, and folio, line, and page milestones, but omits the substantial German Anmerkungen that continue through p. 140. Kölbing transcribed the manuscript directly and compared the English text with Visio Pauli Redaction IV as printed by Herman Brandes in 1885; Brandes's printing was a modern comparison source, not the unknown physical Latin exemplar used by the medieval translator. The available Internet Archive volume is a complete scan of Johnson's 1965 reprint of Englische Studien 22, not an original 1896 carrier, so the reprint and scan are modeled separately. Complete public carriers for Kölbing's article and both Brandes sources have been cached. No public full facsimile of Add MS 10036 was located as of 2026-07-14; current catalog, reading-room, enquiry, and paid whole-object reproduction routes are recorded instead.
review_notes: Middle English is the language of the medieval work. German belongs to Kölbing's modern introduction and commentary; Latin belongs to the related source version and comparative apparatus.
Kölbing is the 1896 editor and transcriber, not the medieval author, despite the U-M item-level author field inherited from the article citation.

The modern BL collection record and specialist catalogs date Add MS 10036 to the fifteenth century or c. 1400-1425. Kölbing's historical fourteenth-century description is retained as a conflict, not silently adopted.
Warwickshire is a scribal-dialect localization for the witness, not a place-of-composition claim.
The Internet Archive Englische Studien 22 PDF is a 1965 reprint scan. Original 1896 publication, 1965 reprint, and modern digital facsimile remain distinct entities.

entities.label: Anonymous Middle English prose Vision of St. Paul represented by Add MS 10036
alternate_labels: A questioun of þe peynes of helle & how soules desireþ to haue rest in þat place
entities.description: Abstract medieval work/version kept distinct from its physical witness, modern editions, and digital encodings. It is a Middle English prose translation and adaptation of the Visio Pauli Redaction IV tradition.

date_statements.label: Translation or adaptation
date_statements.value: By the first quarter of the fifteenth century (terminus ante quem c. 1400-1425)
date_statements.notes: The witness date supplies a terminus ante quem, not an exact composition date.

notes: No current witness census was located that proves Add MS 10036 is the sole surviving witness of this exact version.

entities.label: Eugen Kölbing, Eine bisher unbekannte me. Version von Pauli Höllenfahrt (1896)

date_statements.label: Publication
date_statements.notes: The article is signed Breslau, October 1895, but published in the 1896 volume.

bibliographic.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Eine bisher unbekannte me. Version von Pauli Höllenfahrt,' Englische Studien 22 (Leipzig: O. R. Reisland, 1896), 134-140.
bibliographic.title: Eine bisher unbekannte me. Version von Pauli Höllenfahrt

notes: Kölbing states that this Middle English prose version is printed here for the first time.
He described it in 1896 as the sixth known Middle English version; that historical count is not substituted for a current witness or version census.
CME00151 includes the title, introduction, text, and six inline notes on pp. 134-137 but omits the Anmerkungen after source line 101 through p. 140.

identifiers.scheme: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record

date_statements.label: Specialist catalog date
date_statements.value: Beginning of the fifteenth century / c. 1400-1425

date_statements.label: Historical Kölbing date

holding.notes: Manuscripts are consulted at the British Library in London; the volume was purchased in 1836.

physical_description.extent: v + 100 + ii leaves; modern paper flyleaves
physical_description.format: Octavo manuscript miscellany; single column, 24 lines
notes: One Anglicana Formata scribe; red two-line initials and verse initials.
Modern blue-cloth binding with green leather spine; modern pencil foliation.
The target occupies ff. 81r-85r, crossing from quire 10 (ff. 77r-84v) into quire 11; the following Three Arrows item also begins on f. 85r.
Origin and pre-1836 provenance are not established in the consulted records.

entities.label: Visio Pauli, Redaction IV
alternate_labels: Apocalypse of Paul, Latin Redaction IV
entities.description: Abstract Latin source version against which Kölbing identifies and compares the Middle English prose version. It is not Brandes's 1885 printed artifact and no physical Latin exemplar used by the medieval translator has been identified.

bibliographic.citation: Herman Brandes, Visio S. Pauli: Ein Beitrag zur Visionslitteratur, mit einem deutschen und zwei lateinischen Texten (Halle: Max Niemeyer, 1885).

access.access_method: Request the manuscript for consultation in the British Library London reading rooms using the current archives and manuscripts request form; reader registration and collection conditions apply.
Submit the BL digitisation order form and request a complete current-object color photographic surrogate, subject to conservation assessment, feasibility, service terms, and quotation.

local_copies.label: British Library price list published in 2026 using 2025/26 rates
notes: This is a service-pricing document, not a manuscript facsimile. A complete manuscript order may require individual quotation.

access.contact: permissions@bl.uk for separate reproduction or publication permission questions
access.format: Commissioned digital photography; color and exact capture requirements must be specified
notes: Request the complete current physical object: covers or boards, spine where photographable, pastedowns, every flyleaf and blank, both sides of ff. 1-100, stubs or detached matter, and edges where offered; ask for an image inventory and explicit omissions.
Associate CME00151 separately with ff. 81r-85r; do not crop the whole-object request to the work portion.
The following Three Arrows text also begins on f. 85r, so the terminal leaf should be captured in full.

BL says original manuscripts require expert photography and large or difficult orders may be discussed individually. Request color explicitly, especially if any existing surrogate or microfilm would otherwise be supplied in black and white.
Supply of images and permission to reproduce or publish them are separate questions under the imaging terms.

access.access_method: Use the BL Reference Services enquiry form to ask whether a complete existing color surrogate or microfilm exists and whether it can be supplied.

No working public BL IIIF manifest or digitized-manuscript listing for the shelfmark was located; use the enquiry or paid reproduction routes rather than treating this dated negative finding as proof that no internal surrogate exists.

local_copies.label: British Library digitised manuscripts list, updated 2025-10-10
notes: Add MS 10036 was not found in this list. The file is discovery evidence, not a manuscript facsimile.
The Digirati ARK endpoint returned HTTP 403 and both tested iiif.bl.uk forms returned HTTP 404 on 2026-07-14. These responses are dated access evidence, not a permanent non-digitization conclusion.
The current BL catalog record exposes no digitized-content URL and its LARK/SLARK fields are blank.

rights.component: Repository XML transcription and markup as distinct from repository software
rights.basis: The pinned CME LICENSE uses the Unlicense but describes its scope as software; the manifest does not broaden that wording to inherited U-M transcription, markup, or historical editorial content.
notes: Underlying medieval and 1896 text layers are separately assessed; this record concerns the repository artifact layer.

rights.component: U-M CME digital item and encoded historical text as presented

rights.component: Medieval Middle English text as an abstract work
rights.basis: The anonymous work is attested in a manuscript of c. 1400-1425 and predates modern copyright by centuries.
notes: This does not determine rights in modern transcriptions, editorial apparatus, catalog text, or manuscript photography.

rights.component: Medieval Latin source version as an abstract work
rights.basis: The Visio Pauli is a medieval work whose text predates modern copyright by centuries.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations and contracted letters or syllables
editorial_practices.representation: Expanded material printed in italics; Kölbing notes that manuscript wiþ is consistently represented as w with superscript t
editorial_practices.description: Kölbing explicitly marks expanded letters and syllables typographically rather than presenting an unmarked diplomatic expansion.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English text on pp. 134-137, lines 1-101

editorial_practices.description: Kölbing says punctuation is regularized while the wording otherwise follows the manuscript closely.
Kölbing explicitly capitalizes proper names in the edition.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Witness rubric and red manuscript decoration
editorial_practices.representation: Rubric wording transcribed in typographic print; color is not reproduced
editorial_practices.description: The printed edition and CME encoding preserve the rubric text but not the manuscript's red pigment as facsimile evidence.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Middle English readings relative to Latin Visio Pauli Redaction IV
editorial_practices.representation: Comparison with Brandes's 1885 printed Latin text, especially in the introduction and Anmerkungen
editorial_practices.description: Kölbing uses Brandes's Redaction IV to characterize the English as a fairly faithful translation and to annotate correspondences and divergences.
editorial_practices.scope: Article pp. 134-140; CME00151 retains the introduction and six inline notes but omits most post-text commentary

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete Kölbing article on printed pp. 134-140
editorial_practices.representation: Title, short introduction, Middle English text, six inline notes, and milestones on pp. 134-137 encoded; substantial Anmerkungen on pp. 137-140 and the Breslau October 1895 signature omitted
editorial_practices.description: The digital encoding is complete for Kölbing's edited medieval text but partial for the source article as a whole.

evidence.citation: Offline structural and textual audit of CME00151.xml against the complete Kölbing article scan.
evidence.locator: 1 DIV1; 2 DIV2; 2 HEAD; 4 P; 6 NOTE; 29 MILESTONE; 4 PB; source lines 1-101; folio milestones 81a-85a
evidence.summary: Confirms that the XML represents Kölbing's title, introduction, and complete edited Middle English text on pp. 134-137 but not the substantial Anmerkungen after line 101 through p. 140. The folio milestones map 81a-85a to ff. 81r-85r.

open_questions.question: Which physical Latin manuscript, recension witness, or intermediary did the anonymous Middle English translator use?
next_steps: Collate distinctive Add MS 10036 readings against current Visio Pauli Redaction IV editions and Latin witnesses.
Do not treat Brandes's 1885 edition as the medieval exemplar; it is Kölbing's modern comparison source.
Search IPMEP and specialist Visio Pauli scholarship for a more exact source classification.

open_questions.question: How should Kölbing's fourteenth-century dating and DMBR's abbreviated folios 81-84 be reconciled with modern c. 1400-1425 dating and the exact ff. 81r-85r locus?
next_steps: Inspect a complete color facsimile of Add MS 10036, especially the quire transition and shared f. 85r boundary.
Consult the latest BL curatorial or IPMEP description for the basis of the current date.
Continue to preserve both historical claims while preferring modern specialist dating and side-specific foliation.

open_questions.question: Does the British Library hold an existing complete color surrogate or microfilm of Add MS 10036 that is not exposed through the public catalog or IIIF endpoints?
next_steps: Ask Reference Services whether a complete surrogate or microfilm exists, its exact coverage and color status, and whether it can be supplied.
If none exists, request a quote for complete current-object color photography with a delivered image inventory and explicit omissions.
Recheck the BL catalog, digitized-manuscript list, and public IIIF endpoints after any platform migration or curatorial response.
notes: No public full manuscript facsimile was cached because none was located.

open_questions.question: Did U-M key CME00151 from an original 1896 printing, the 1965 Johnson reprint, or another page-identical copy?
next_steps: Inspect any U-M project production record or source-copy metadata for CME00151.
Do not infer the physical source carrier solely from unchanged original pagination.

open_questions.question: Is Add MS 10036 the only surviving witness of this exact Middle English prose version?
next_steps: Check current IPMEP and Visio Pauli witness bibliographies under IPMEP 535, the rubric, incipit, and explicit.
Avoid a unique-witness assertion until a current census is verified.
